coredata.dc:title: Biogenic approach for fabricating biocompatible carbon dots and their application in colorimetric and fluorometric sensing of lead ion
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, Pooja
Chaudhary, Savita
Kumar, Rajeev
coredata.dc:description: The rapid growth in the industrial and urban sector has filled enormous stress on environment. The exponential use of chemicals especially metal ions has raised the pollution index and affected the health of living beings. Therefore, it is highly essential need to monitor and remove these metal ion toxins by the advanced materials which itself non-lethal, bio-congenial and easily workable with high selectivity and sensitivity. To solve these issues, the current study proposed the efficient utilization of Pearl Millet seeds into value-added carbon dots (C-dots) via using simple thermal treatment. Engaging agricultural product as a starting precursor for C-dots synthesis made the process economically more viable. The basic structural properties (size ∼ 4–5 nm, shape ∼ spherical, and crystalline nature) were assessed through HRTEM, PXRD, FTIR and EDX analysis. The optical and luminescence properties were appraised by investigating the variation in the absorbance and emission intensity as a function of pH (2–12) and excitation wavelength (245 nm–305 nm). The developed strategy has provided the better control over the aqueous solubility, quantum yield (52%) and optical properties of C-dots. The high loading amounts of C-dots ranging from 100 to 5000 mg/L has produced a significant enhancement in the biocompatibility rate of C-dots against bacterial strains, fungus, algae, chromosomal compatibility on Allium cepa and seeds germination activity of Vigna radiate. The strong fluorescence emission and optical properties of as prepared C-dots made them efficient for selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ ion (detection limit = 0.18 nM) from waste water via using colorimetric, UV–vis. absorption and fluorescence based sensory methods. The development sensor performed well in water obtained from different natural resources with 90% average recovery rate. These findings would make the economically feasible C-dots as an effective material for wastewater treatment via overcoming the barriers of low selectivity and advanced the implementation of the process on large scale. Furthermore, the usage of biowaste as starting precursor offers a solution for ecological burdens while synchronically reducing the pernicious effect of the waste on the ecosystem and cleans up the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal ion
Toxicity
Biocompatibility
Pearl millet
Colorimetric sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and physicochemical characterization of C-dots
Reviewing of biocompatibility of C-dots by using multiple assays
Reviewing of metal ion sensing behaviour of PNP@C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images and corresponding SAED pattern of C-dots (b) PNP@C-dots (c) FTIR spectrum (d) XRD spectrum of C-dots.
Fluorescence spectra of C-dots (a) as a function of different excitation wavelength and (b) variation in pH of the reaction media with λex = 250 nm.
The effect of different concentrations of C-dots on the (a) Antimicrobial (b) Antifungal and (c) percentage fungus growth activities.
(a) Digital images and (b) respective tabulated values of average root length, PIG, Germination % and GI of Vigna radiate seed in presence of different concentrations of C-dots.
The optical images of different mitotic stages in A. cepa root tip in case of C-dots.
The digital images showing the effect of different concentration of C-dot on algal growth by using paper disc method.
(a) UV–visible and (b) Fluorescence spectra of PNP@C-dots in presence of different metal ions.
Schematic illustration showing the sensing mechanism of PNP@C-dot in presence of Pb2+ ions.
(a) PL spectra and inset graph is stern-Volmer plot for the respective metal ion (b) UV–vis. spectra showing the concentration effect of Pb2+ in presence of PNP@ C-dots.

The emission profile showing the effect of interfering ions for (a) Pb2+ ions (b) Normalized intensity plot of emission profile and (c) binding constant between PNP@C-dot and Pb2+ has been obtained from Benesi-Hilderbrand plot (inset).
Percentage recovery of emission intensity for Pb2+ ion in different water sources.
The respective values of phase index, CAI and Chromosomal aberrations in presence of different concentration of C-dots in A. cepa root tip.
Representation of corresponding values of slope, regression coefficient, standard deviation, detection limit and quantification limit of PNP@C-dots in presence of Pb2+ ion.

head.ce:title: Biogenic approach for fabricating biocompatible carbon dots and their application in colorimetric and fluorometric sensing of lead ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environment Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environment Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling editor Prof. Jiri Jaromir Klemeš

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid growth in the industrial and urban sector has filled enormous stress on environment. The exponential use of chemicals especially metal ions has raised the pollution index and affected the health of living beings. Therefore, it is highly essential need to monitor and remove these metal ion toxins by the advanced materials which itself non-lethal, bio-congenial and easily workable with high selectivity and sensitivity. To solve these issues, the current study proposed the efficient utilization of Pearl Millet seeds into value-added carbon dots (C-dots) via using simple thermal treatment. Engaging agricultural product as a starting precursor for C-dots synthesis made the process economically more viable. The basic structural properties (size ∼ 4–5 nm, shape ∼ spherical, and crystalline nature) were assessed through HRTEM, PXRD, FTIR and EDX analysis. The optical and luminescence properties were appraised by investigating the variation in the absorbance and emission intensity as a function of pH (2–12) and excitation wavelength (245 nm–305 nm). The developed strategy has provided the better control over the aqueous solubility, quantum yield (52%) and optical properties of C-dots. The high loading amounts of C-dots ranging from 100 to 5000 mg/L has produced a significant enhancement in the biocompatibility rate of C-dots against bacterial strains, fungus, algae, chromosomal compatibility on Allium cepa and seeds germination activity of Vigna radiate. The strong fluorescence emission and optical properties of as prepared C-dots made them efficient for selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ ion (detection limit = 0.18 nM) from waste water via using colorimetric, UV–vis. absorption and fluorescence based sensory methods. The development sensor performed well in water obtained from different natural resources with 90% average recovery rate. These findings would make the economically feasible C-dots as an effective material for wastewater treatment via overcoming the barriers of low selectivity and advanced the implementation of the process on large scale. Furthermore, the usage of biowaste as starting precursor offers a solution for ecological burdens while synchronically reducing the pernicious effect of the waste on the ecosystem and cleans up the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: The unprocessed release of harmful toxins especially heavy metal ions from industries, laboratories, agricultural and domestic sectors possesses a major environmental concern all over the world (Zhao and Liu, 2019). The carelessness in the management of waste water disposal in developing countries like India is another major contributing factor in heavy metal ion pollution in water resources (Gautam et al., 2020). The high toxicity with non-biodegradable and adhering nature of heavy metal ion towards groundwater and soil resources have caused severe health hazards to living beings even in low concentration (Liu et al., 2019). Out of different metal ions, the primary recognized toxic element is lead (Pb) by the mankind (Wang et al., 2019). According to United State Environmental protection agency (USEPA), the permissible limit of lead ion is 15 μg/L in drinking water (Pan et al., 2019). Numerous scientific assessments affirmed that Pb is producing neurotoxicity and can cause lung embolisms and kidney disorders in living beings (Nath et al., 2015). The lethal impact of Pb can produce severe health related issues (Gangemi et al., 2018). Therefore, the scientific community is in a run to discover innovative means to detect the presence of Pb2+ ions in natural resources and minimize their contamination rate (Qian et al., 2015). Various methods comprising ICP-MS, AAS, FAAS and WXRF are used for the detection of Pb2+ ions ( Senthilkumar and Saraswathi, 2009; Soylak et al., 2005, Wu and Boyle, 1997). However, the costly and complicated multistep processing has restricted their widespread usage in metal ion detection (Cui et al., 2015). Therefore, it is highly required need to monitor and remove these metal ion toxins by the advanced materials which itself non-lethal, bio-congenial and easily workable with high selectivity and sensitivity. From this prospective, the optimized physicochemical and surface properties of nanomaterials have made them relevant material for preparing sensor ensembles for scrutinizing different metal ions in aqueous media (Chaudhary et al., 2019). However, many of used nanomaterials have shown lower efficiency in the large-scale processing and displayed reduced affinity to detect low concentrations of metal ions in aqueous media (Su et al., 2019).

In this alliance, C-dots have received high consideration and displayed significant usage in waste water treatment (Sabet and Mahdavi, 2019). On comparing with the commonly used nanoparticles in the field of sensing, it is presumed that the biocompatible nature with superior optical and luminescence properties of C-dots has boosted their application in metal ion sensing (Boruah and Biswas, 2018). The higher adsorption rate with advanced electrical conductivity has made C-dots as effective material in waste water treatment (Kaur et al., 2019). The existence of high photo stability with better control over aqueous solubility made C-dots as valuable sensing probe in bioimaging (Angamuthu et al., 2018). Till date, various methodologies including hydrothermal treatment, microwave assisted synthesis, ball milling methods, combustion, pyrolysis, arc discharge methods have been employed for the synthesis of C-dots (Namdari et al., 2017). Though, the probable environmental concerns associated with the used synthetic methodology have not been taken care effectively. The usage of conventional chemicals during their synthesis and sophisticated instrumentations has possessed additional cost and toxic impact over their application in waste water treatment (Wee et al., 2013). For instance, Alam et al. (2019) have used the physicochemical process for the fabrication of C-dots by using β-cyclodextrin as templating agent. The formed particles have displayed high selectivity towards Pb (II) ions with detection limit of 0.0023 mg/L using expensive differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry technique. The usage of conventional organic solvents such as DMF, DMAP, DCC have further restricted their scale up application in waste water treatment.

In this lieu, green methodologies for the synthesis of C-dots by using biomass would consider as a more desirable alternative to existing techniques (Chauhan et al., 2020). Different types of natural biomass products comprising tea extract, milk, honey, vegetable and fruit waste, sugar have been used for the synthesis of C-dots. The noxious obstacle has easily eradicated by using green synthetic procedure for the clean production of C-dots (Zhou et al., 2015). In addition, the comparative effect on different living species has given potential prospective of C-dots in environmental remediation activities (Bano et al., 2018).

The current study hereby reported the efficient utilization of Pearl Millet seeds into value-added carbon dots (C-dots) via using simple thermal treatment. The primary benefit of the used strategy is an absence of conventional solvents during synthesis. The water has been used as a reaction medium for the transformation of Pearl Millet seeds into highly fluorescent C-dots. In addition, Pearl Millet seeds is one of the primary food crops that grown more widely worldwide. The employed strategy has not only controlled the biocompatibility parameters of C-dots but has produced direct advancements for curing environmental worries and sustainability concerns in cost effective manner. The optical, luminescence, thermal and biocompatibility properties of C-dots have displayed important effect of the starting material. The starting source has also played significant effect on the size and shape of C-dots. The developed strategy has provided better control over the aqueous solubility and quantum yield (52%) of prepared C-dots. To the best of our perception, the current work should represent the one of its kind efforts to examine the effect of high loading amounts of C-dots ranging from 100 to 5000 mg/L against bacterial strains, fungus, algae, chromosomal compatibility on Allium cepa and seeds germination activity of Vigna radiate. The strong fluorescence emission and optical properties of as prepared C-dots made them efficient sensor for selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ ion from waste water samples. Based on the aforementioned advantages, the developed methodology has offered an efficient economically viable tool for the detecting highly toxic Pb ion via overcoming the barriers of low selectivity and advanced the implementation of the current strategy to real water samples on large scale.

The healthy seeds of Pearl Millet, Vigna radiate and Allium cepa (onion) for genotoxicity testing were acquired from the local market (Sector 15, Chandigarh, India). P-Nitrophenol (PNP) with 99% purity was obtained from Sigma Aldrich. The respective analytical grade of nitrate salt of Ag+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, NH4 +, Na+ and K+ were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The doubly distilled water was used for all sample preparation. Muller Hinton agar, Bold’s basal media (BBM-10X) and Trichloro acetic acid (TCA) were obtained from Himedia Pvt. Ltd (India). Methylene blue and mercury chloride were obtained from Fischer Scientific and Emplura respectively. An isolated colony of Laurilia taxodii and algal culture of Chlorella was provided by Department of Environmental Studies and Department of Botany, Panjab University Chandigarh respectively. The microbial cultures of Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella typhi and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were obtained from Department of Microbiology, Panjab University Chandigarh. Quinine sulphate (Sigma Aldrich) was employed for estimating the quantum yield of the prepared C-dots (Chaudhary et al., 2018, 2016).

The seeds of Pearl Millet as the starting precursor were used for the greener synthesis of C-dots. Primarily, the acquired seeds were properly washed and then placed in the drying chamber for removing moisture content at 70 °C for 6 h (Chaudhary et al., 2016). Then, 20 gm of the Pearl Millet seeds were placed in a muffle furnace under nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h at 350 °C. Around 17 gm of black powder was retrieved after thermal calcination. Afterwards, 1.5 gm of grinded black powder was dissolved in 50 ml of double distilled water and stirred for 24 h. The obtained black solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered by using 0.22 μm filter membrane. The existing impurities were removed by filtering the samples through dialysis bag of 1000 MWCO. The obtained C-dots were stored at 4 °C for further analysis. For the surface modulation, equivalent volume of 5 mM PNP and 1.5 gm aqueous solution of C-dots was mixed under stirring conditions. The quantum yield (ɸ) of prepared C-dots was calculated by taking quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference standard (ɸF) (eq. (1)).
(1)
Φ
=
x
Φ r
I x
I r
(
η x
η r
)
2
A r
A x
Where ɸ is quantum yield, I is measured integrated emission intensity, ɳ is the refractive index (for aqueous solution ɳ = 1), A is the optical density values, x refers to the test sample and r refers to the reference sample.

The muffle furnace from AICIL was used for the preparation of C-dots. Remi R-24 was utilized for ultra-centrifugation of synthesized C-dots. Mettler Toledo digital pH meter was used to study the effect of pH on C-dots. IKA C-MAG HS7 instrument was used for magnetic stirring of prepared samples. The optical absorption spectroscopic analysis was done on LABINDIA UV–Vis. spectrophotometer. PerkinElmer LS 55 PL spectrophotometer was used for the fluorescence studies. Panalytical X’Pert Pro XRD powder diffractometer with monochromatic CuKα radiation over the 2θ range of 5–90° with scan rate of 2 deg min−1 was used for the analysis of crystallographic structure of C-dots. Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (PerkinElmer (RX1)) in the working range of 4000–400 cm−1 was used for the identification of functional group over the surface of C-dots. The size distribution profile was analyzed by using the Malvern (ZEN 1690) Dynamic light scattering instrument. Nikon ECLIPSE E100 light microscope with attached camera was used for the analysis of chromosomal aberration in A. cepa in presence of C-dots. Differential scanning calorimetry (METTLER TOLEDO (Q20)) was used to measure the change in heat capacity of sample. Tecnai G2-20 (FSI) S-Twin High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used for topological study.

Agar well diffusion method (Sobierajska et al., 2018) was used to evaluate the antimicrobial activity of C-dots with different concentration ranges. For the analysis, standard strains of both gram positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and gram negative (Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi & Pseudomonas aeruginosa) bacteria stored in nutrient broth were utilized as a model strain. Briefly discussing, Muller Hinton Agar was initially poured in disposable petri plates and allowed to stand for gel formation (Kumar et al., 2017). The solution of bacterial culture was dispersed uniformly over agar gel for the analysis. The 5 mm wells were formed by sterilized stainless steel cork-borer. The procedure was performed in triplicates and average minimum inhibition value was noted for further analysis. The overnight culturing of isolated colony of Laurilia taxodii fungus was done at 18 °C in agar media for carrying out the antifungal activity of C-dots. The different concentrations of C-dots were prepared in double distilled water by using Malt Extract (MEA) powder and Agar-Agar Type-1 media. The formed solution was autoclaved along with C-dots sample to remove all type of contamination. Afterwards, media was allowed to cool down and placed in disposable petri plates (Chaudhary et al., 2017). The mixture was allowed to stand overnight in laminar air flow for solidification. The respective blank control plate was prepared by using same volume of media and distilled water as substituted sample. The fungus mycelia with approximate diameter of 1 mm was transferred over formed gel in petri plates and placed in incubator at 25 °C for 5 days to check the zone of fungus grown in every case (Chen et al., 2018). The respective antifungal index was calculated by using eq. (2).
(2)
( % )
A n t i f u n g a l I n d e x
=
(
1 −
D a
D b
)
x
100
Where Da is the diameter of zone of fungus growth in test samples and Db is the diameter of zone of fungus growth in control sample. The experiments were performed in triplicate to check the reliability of same samples.

The respective effect of C-dots was further examined on the seed germination rate of Vigna radiata at room temperature conditions for enhancing their application scope in the agriculture field. The influence of darkness vs. light condition on the germination of Vigna radiata was also investigated in presence of C-dots. The results have provided the inside to the anti-proliferative and cyto-toxic effects of C-dots on Vigna radiata. For the analysis, good quality seeds were thoroughly washed with distilled water and followed by washing with 0.1% of mercury chloride solution. Afterwards, the seeds were again washed with distilled water for thorough cleaning. In order to investigate the influence of different concentrations of C-dots, approximately equal number of seeds were sown in petri dishes and allowed to soak in different concentration solution of C-dots. For control system, seeds were soaked in distilled water. The respective petri dishes were placed in warm and dark condition for 2–3 days for germination of seeds. This experiment was performed in triplicates and average lengths of the germinated seeds were taken to minimize the experimental error. The seedling physiological indexes were measured in terms of percentage inhibition growth (PIG).
(3)
P I G
=
(
L C − L N
L C
)
Where LC is average length of control seeds and LN is average length of seeds in a particular concentration of C-dot solutions. The germination % was calculated for each set by using eq. (4):
(4)
G e r min a t i o n
( % )
=
(
N u m b e r
o f
g e r min a t e d
s e e d
T o t a l
n u m b e r
o f
s e e d s
)
x 100

The Germination Index was calculated for each set in accordance to eq. (5):
(5)
G I
=
(
N t
x
L t
N c
x
L c
)
x 100
Where Nc and Nt is count of seeds germinated for control and each treatment respectively. On the other hand, Lc and Lt is mean of root length of seeds germinated after incubation in control and for each treatment (Chaudhary et al., 2016).

The anti-algal activity of C-dots was examined by using the paper disc method. For the examination, good quality soil was taken and autoclaved along with the BBM solution. Freshly cultured algae species of Chlorella was grown in Erlenmeyer flask with BBM solution at room temperature (Sharma et al., 2019). A 96 well culture plate was used for this study. Each well was filled 1/3rd with sand and 100 μl of different concentration solution of C-dots were poured in each well. Subsequently paper disc of about 5 mm diameter was placed over each well (Sharma et al., 2019). Around 10 μl of algal culture solution was poured over each disc and was placed under 16:8 light and dark condition for 8 consecutive days. After 8th day, the growth of algae was measured over each paper disc. The extracted chlorophyll from algae was analyzed by UV–vis. spectroscopic measurements (Chaudhary et al., 2018). The genital level study of C-dots was done by using A. cepa chromosomal aberration assay. For the analysis, healthy onion bulbs were grown in the direct contact of C-dots at room temperature. Five freshly grown root tips from each treatment was taken and fixed for mitotic activity analysis. The freshly grown roots were cut with the help of sterilized scissor and placed in 1N HCl solution (de Andrade et al., 2014). The solution was incubated at 60 °C for 12–14 min. Afterwards, processed roots were washed with distilled water and transferred to the glass slide and stained with methylene blue dye. The different mitotic stages were studied under light microscope. The consequential effect of different concentrations of C-dots can explicate on A. cepa by measuring the following parameters (Modlitbová et al., 2018).
(6)
M i t o t i c
I n d e x
( M I )
=
(
P + M + A + T
T o t a l
n u m b e r
o f
c e l l s
i n
int e r p h a s e
)
(7)
Re l a t i v e
M i t o t i c
I n d e x
( M I )
=
(
M I
M
I c
)
(8)
C h r o m o s o m a l
A b e r r a t i o n
I n d e x
( C A I )
=
(
C A
T D
)
(9)
P h a s e
I n d i c i e s
( P I )
=
(
T
x
c
T
D C
)
x 100
(10)
T o t a l
%
o f
A b n o r m a l
C e l l s =
(
T
a b n
T
D c
)
x 100
Where P + M + A + T = total number of prophases, metaphase, anaphase and telophase. MI refers to the mitotic index of a particular treatment and MIC represents the MI value for the control system. CA represents the number of divisions with chromosomal aberration and TD represents the total number of divisions in A. cepa. PI is phase index; Tc is total number of cells observed and TDc is total number of dividing cells and Tabn is total number of abnormal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and physicochemical characterization of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM images of the C-dots and PNP@C-dots have shown spherical particles with diameter of 4–5 nm (Fig. 1 a and b). The lattice fringes and circular rings in SAED patterns have represented the formation of polycrystalline nature in C-dots (inset Fig. 1a). Whereas, small circular rings with few bright spots have depicted the origin of crystalline nature of C-dots in presence of PNP (inset Fig. 1b). EDX spectrum of C-dots and PNP@C-dots has shown the presence of characteristic peaks for C, N and O (Fig. S1, supplementary data). The results have clearly suggested the purity of formed C-dots from Pearl Millet seeds. The calculated hydrodynamic Zaverage size (d. nm) for C-dots was 15–20 nm with polydispersity index value of 0.31. Whereas the Zaverage value for PNP@ C-dots was 17–25 nm with polydispersity index of 0.28 respectively. The bonding linkages of PNP with C-dots were visualized by using FTIR analysis (Fig. 1c). The = C–H stretching peak was observed at 3191 cm−1 for C-dots. The O–H stretching peak in carboxylic acid group was observed at 2756 cm−1. The peak at 2262 cm−1 was for C≡N group, 2120 cm−1 for –C≡C- stretching, 1417 cm−1 for C–H bending mode in C-dots. The C–O bond stretching was observed at 1191 cm−1 for C-dots. In case of PNP, the major peaks were observed at 3200, 2914, 2884, 2794 cm−1 for C–H stretching, C–H bond in alkane and O–H stretching peaks respectively. The peaks at 2259, 2125, 1279, 1240, 1145, 1104 cm−1 were corresponded to the C–O stretching. The peak at 1658 cm−1 was associated with the C=C alkenes, 1465 cm−1 for asymmetric C–H bending, 1359 cm−1 for N=O valence asymmetric and symmetric bending mode in PNP. The phenolic –OH peak was observed at 1342 cm−1. The aromatic in plane C–H bending mode was observed at 1060 cm−1. The peak at 961 cm−1 was associated for the –OH bending mode. The peak at 841 cm−1 was associated for the non-polar deformation vibration in C–H bonds of aromatic ring. The respective peaks at 3469 cm−1 was associated for –OH alcohol and 2956 cm−1 for C–H stretching respectively. The other peaks at 2916, 2872, 2848, 1467, 827 cm−1 were corresponded to the –CH2 stretching vibration and –CH2 out of plane bending vibration in case of C-dots and PNP@C-dot. The peak at 1655 cm−1 for C–C stretching, 1378 cm−1 for C–N stretch, 991 cm−1 for O–H bonding in case of PNP@C-dots. The = C–H bending was observed at 917 cm−1. From FTIR results, it can be inferred that developed C-dots have desired functional groups which further enhance solubility and complexation ability with metal ions and increased their scope in sensing applications. The DSC thermal analysis of C-dots has shown the existence of only one exothermic peak with heat of enthalpy and transition temperature values of 270 J/g and 109.46 °C respectively (Fig. S2).

Fig. 1d has displayed the XRD patterns and respective information about the crystallinity for C-dots, PNP and PNP@C-dots respectively. In all cases, the sharp and intense peaks were obtained due to the crystalline nature of formed C-dots. The major diffraction peak for C-dots were observed at 2θ = 27.97°, 29.47°, 31.39°, 40.21°, 45.06° and 49.82° which were corresponded to the (112), (101), (311), (111), (200) and (102) planes. The respective XRD peak at 27.97° was mainly generated due to the presence of inter-planar distance between the graphitic layers in C-dots (Chauhan et al., 2020). Whereas in case of PNP, the diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 17.90°, 21.38°, 25.41°, 26.50° and 27.39° which were corresponded to the (101), (112), (202), (112) and (112) planes. In case of PNP@C-dots, the diffraction peaks were observed at 17.01°, 19.95°, 24.23°, 25.84° and 28.21° corresponded to the (101), (100), (112), (202) and (112) plane. The crystalline sizes of C-dots, PNP and PNP@C-dots was 15.4, 24.5 and 17.1 nm respectively and comparable with the DLS results.

The absorption and emission spectra were recorded to analyze the optical and fluorescence properties of as synthesized C-dots. The UV–vis. spectra have shown a broad characteristic peak at 312 nm for the prepared C-dots. The peak was mainly prescribed for the n-ᴨ∗ transition of N or O containing bond (C=O and C=N) in C-dots (Fig. S3a). The shoulder peak at around 215 nm has been corresponded to the ᴨ-ᴨ∗ transition for C=C bond in C-dots (Pal et al., 2018). The respective absorption peaks for PNP and PNP@C-dots was observed at 405 nm and at 454 nm respectively. In case of PNP, the peak has been shifted toward longer wavelength in comparison to C-dots. This was mainly explained due to the presence of auxochromic group (-OH) in PNP. These groups have the tendency to increase the intensity and wavelength shift in the absorption spectra of PNP. The bathochromic shift of peak in PNP@C-dot has clearly explained the existence of strong complex formation between the amine group (-NH2) of C-dot and phenolic group (-OH) of PNP. The corresponding emission spectra of C-dots were studied as a function of excitation wavelength ranging from 245 nm to 305 nm with maximum emission intensity was observed for 250 nm (Fig. 2 a). The C-dots have shown significant peak at 415 nm with a quantum yield of 52%. The prominent decrease in emission intensity of C-dots was seen by exciting the particles between 255 nm and 305 nm. The particular excitation-based changes in the emission intensity of C-dots have been explained on the basis of variations in the obtained particle sizes and changes in the emissive sites over the surface of C-dots. In case of PNP, the respective emission peak was observed at 501 nm (Fig. S3b). The shift in the peak position has been explained due to the presence of different chromophoric group in PNP molecules which further altered the value of dielectric constant. On the other hand, PNP@C-dots has displayed the peak positioned at 505 nm (Fig. S3b). For enhancing the practical applications of C-dots in sensing, the detail analysis of their emission intensities has been visualized at different pH values of the reaction media (Fig. 2b). From the data in Fig. 2b, it has been interpreted that on increasing the value of pH from 2 to 10, the emission intensity of C-dots has significantly increased. At pH = 12, the emission intensity was found to be maximum (around 40% higher than pH = 10) with simultaneous hypsochromic (blue) shift in the peak position. This behavioural variation was associated with the changes in the carboxyl groups present on the outer surface of C-dots in the basic media. These variations were further associated with the elimination of emissive states on the outer surface of C-dots. In addition, the photoluminescence intensity of C-dots was found to be similar up to 15 weeks and supported the stability of formed C-dots in varying environmental conditions of reaction media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reviewing of biocompatibility of C-dots by using multiple assays
ce:section.ce:para: The high loading amounts of C-dots ranging from 100 mg to 5000 mg/L were tested against standard bacterial strains of Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella typhi and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Fig. 3 a). There was no zone of inhibition in case of control experiment with respect to all chosen bacterial strains (Fig. 3a). The existence of minimum value of zone of inhibition in presence of C-dots for all bacterial strains has further supported the biocompatible nature of C-dots (Table S1). In addition, the increasing amount of concentration of C-dots with respective to control system has not displayed any kind of zone of inhibition in chosen bacterial strains. The existence of carboxyl group on the exterior surface of C-dots has made the particles soluble in aqueous media and provided biocompatibility to the formed particles. The cellular channels and bacterial osmoregulation process of bacterial strains was unaffected in presence of C-dots and helped in promoting the bacterial growth.

To further compliments the antimicrobial studies of C-dots, the corresponding effect of same loading concentrations (100 mg–5000 mg/L) of C-were tested over Laurilia taxodii fungus (Fig. 3b). In case of control experiment, fungus was grown to 9 cm in diameter which was exactly similar to growth of fungus in presence of 100 mg/L concentration solution of C-dot (Table S1). The enhancement in the concentration of C-dots from 100 mg/L to 5000 mg/L has not affected the growth rate of fungus (Fig. 3c). The constancy in fungus growth in presence of C-dot was mainly explained due to stable association of carboxyl moieties of C-dots with chitin molecule (primary component of cell wall) in fungus. These results have supported the biocompatibility of the prepared particles and compliments the results obtained from antimicrobial studies. The Vigna radiate as model seeds were chosen to investigate the anti-proliferative activity of C-dots on spore germination. The five different concentrations i.e. 100, 500, 1000, 2500 and 5000 mg/L were employed for the analysis and double distilled water was taken for the control experiment. The average length of root of Vigna radiate was found to be 2.3 cm. Whereas, the length of germinated root varies from 2.3 cm to 2.2 cm as the concentration increased from 500 to 5000 mg/L (Fig. 4 a). The respective values of percentage inhibition growth, percentage germination and germination index were tabulated in Fig. 4b. The enhancement in biocompatibility was found to be 99% with respect to control experiment and supported the antifungal and antibacterial activities in presence of C-dots. The growth of nuclei in plants was explained due to the presence of non-toxic carboxyl moieties around the C-dot surface.

The structure and particle size of formed C-dots have also displayed significant impact over the generation of reactive oxygen species which will further disturbed the cell wall in A. cepa and provided the direct mean to monitor the genotoxic and mutagenic effects of C-dots. The chosen experiment was found to be very sensitive in calculating the impact of C-dots on different stages and chromosomal aberration in each cell (Fig. 5 ). The existence of all types of mitotic stages with absence of chromosomal aberration in presence of different concentrations of C-dots has verified the biocompatibility of chosen particles (Table S1). The observed metaphase, anaphase and telophase in respective concentration of C-dots and control experiment were found to be similar (Fig. 5). The contribution of interphase followed by prophase was found to be maximum in all the chosen concentrations of C-dots.

For instance, MI value for control was 60% and 59% in presence of C-dots. The small size particles of C-dots have the tendency to promote their diffusion across the cell walls. In addition, the dispersed particles of C-dots have tendency to generate free radical in the cells and supported their growth and given direct evidence for compatibility of formed C-dots. However, 5000 mg/L amount of C-dot have shown the existence of chromosomal break and laggard chromosome in A. cepa (Table 1 ). This was mainly explained due to the non absorption of such a high concentration of C-dots by the root tip cells of A. cepa and further has the tendency to disturb the mitotic stages (Fig. 5b).

The biocompatibility of C-dots as a function of concentration was further confirmed over the algae growth by using paper disc method (Fig. 6 ). In presence of 100 mg/L of C-dots, the growth of algae was found to be comparable with the control system. On the other hand, the higher concentrations of C-dots have displayed reduction in the algae growth. It might be due to the enhanced external electron transfer via the carboxyl moieties over the surface of C-dots. The number of light-harvesting complex species in the photosynthesis process was affected in presence of C-dots and leads to the increase in the growth rate of algae. The qualitative estimation of chlorophyll content was done by measuring the absorbance value at 645 and 665 nm. The chlorophyll content of control was taken as 100% and respective measurements of different concentration solution of C-dots were calculated with respect to control (Table S1). The 10% reduction of chlorophyll content in case of 5000 mg/L concentration solution of C-dots was observed in the system. The obtained results were comparable with the earlier studies (antimicrobial, antifungal, anti-proliferative and A. cepa activity) and hence verified the biocompatibility of the prepared C-dots particles. From all these activities, it can be inferred that all the results were in excellent agreement with each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reviewing of metal ion sensing behaviour of PNP@C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Metals have been considered as important constituents in diverse physiological ventures of living beings. Therefore, signalling out of these metal ions in selective manners has been getting so much of interest in the area of research (Jana et al., 2018). In this regard, the fluorescent nature of prepared C-dots has provided a new alternative for the recognition of metal ion in selective and sensitive manner. The delocalisation of conjugated π electron in C-dots allows easy complexation of metal ions with C-dots. The fluorescence and UV–vis. studies of as synthesized C-dots was performed at pH = 7 in presence of Co2+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, NH4 +and Fe2+ ions as their nitrate and chloride salts with concentration of 30 mM and λexc = 250 nm. Fig. 7 a and (b) has shown the UV–visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of PNP@C-dots in the presence of the mentioned metal ions. Out of different types of metal ions, Pb2+ ion have shown maximum enhancement in the absorption and emission intensity in presence of C-dots. A meticulous analysis has shown that sensed metal ion (Pb2+) has displayed distinctive behaviour and two peaks at 486 and 610 nm were observed in presence of PNP@C-dots. The respective ratio 486/610 peaks in presence of Pb2+ was around 4.007 (Fig. 7b).

The yellow color solution of PNP@C-dots remained intact for other metal ions and became colourless in case of Pb2+ ion. Thus, the developed sensor provided the visual color variations for Pb2+ ions and acted as efficient colorimetric senor (Fig. 8 ). The mechanism of colorimetric sensing was described on the basis of modulation of energy charge transfer process and complexation of Pb2+ ions with the PNP@C-dot. In addition, the peak of PNP@C-dot has also shown bathochromic shift in the presence of the Pb2+ in both UV–vis. and fluorescence spectra. This result might be due to the complexation between the frameworks of PNP@C-dot and the sensed metal ion. The –OH group of PNP has the tendency to interact with outer surrounding groups of C-dots (carboxyl and amine groups). The metal ions have the ability to react with the PNP which has shown enhancement in the spectra. These behavioural variations have produced the new absorption and fluorescence turn on sensor for Pb2+ ions in the aqueous media.

Moreover, the increase in the emission intensity in the presence of C-dots has also attributed due to the increase in the radioactive decay life and excitation intrinsic behaviour in C-dots. The enhancement in intensity value was also affected by the presence of local electric field on the fluorophoric group at the surface of PNP which further enhanced the emission rate of photons. The excited fluorophoric group have the ability to transfer energy to the C-dots which further emit radiating plasmon and produces enhancement in the intensity pattern. As the position of peaks has slightly shifted and small new peak was formed which has further suggest that not only physical interaction, but chemical interactions were also taking place in C-dots in presence of Pb2+ ions. But in case of other metal ions, this enhancement was not observed which might be due to the non-complexation and non-aggregation process between metal ions and PNP@C-dot (Fig. 8). From the literature study, it has also been interfered that when complex formation took place, the respective number of vibrational and rotational modes decreased. As a result, band gap has decreased which further enhanced the number of transitions and increased the value of fluorescence intensity (Alizadeh et al., 2009). On comparing with the available data (Table S2), it has been found that the formed particles in this work was quite selective and provided high selectivity and sensitivity towards Pb2+ ions. The respective effect of Pb2+ ion concentration ranging from 1 nM to 30 mM in presence of PNP@C-dot system has also been checked for detailed analysis (Fig. 9 ). The absorbance value has displayed the linear enhancement in the intensity value as a function of concentration. This significant increment in the absorbance value was mainly explained due to increase in concentration of metal ions.

The outcome has further suggested the stable and strong complex formation between metal ion and PNP@C-dot. Similar enhancement in the fluorescence intensity was observed for sensed metal ion in emission spectra and shown linear dependency as a function of different concentration of Pb2+. The calibration curve for the sensed metal ion was plotted for the estimation of quantification limits for metal ions in PNP@C-dot system (Fig. 9 inset). The value of detection limit was calculated by using standard 3σ IUPAC criteria (Table 2 ). The following stern-Volmer relationship was explored to calculate different parameters (Cheng et al., 2019).
(11)
F 0
F
= 1 + Ksv τ
[ Q ]
Where F and F0 have referred to the fluorescence intensity with and without the metal ions, Ksv is the stern-Volmer constant, τ refers to the lifetime of emissive excited state and [Q] refers to the concentration of metal ion.

On comparing with the available literature data, it has been found that the low selectivity with costly processing with multistep synthesis has restricted the widespread and scale up usage of nanomaterials in environmental remediation activities (Table S2). In this regard, the current work offers the simple synthesis of C-dots with high value of quantum yield. The aqueous solubility of C-dots has further enhanced their use in the detection of harmful toxin from many natural resources of water. The as synthesized PNP@C-dots system has also served as a highly selective and intriguing platform towards toxic lead metal ion and harmful pollutants.

In order to evaluate the interference study of developed fluorescent sensor, different metal ions were taken into consideration during the analysis. The respective 30 mM solution of interfering cations were prepared in water and mixed with Pb2+ ion solution having similar concentration in presence of PNP@C-dots (Fig. 10 ). The samples were properly mixed and sonicated before taking the emission spectra. On interpreting the data, it was observed that the emission intensity of PNP@C-dots was unaffected in presence of different metal ions (Fig. S4). The intensity pattern as well as the peak position of Pb2+ ion was found to be unaffected in presence of other interfering ions. Therefore, the prepared system was highly efficient and selective towards Pb2+ ion. The developed system has also provided the comprehensive and collective information for the selective and sensitive sensing of Pb2+ ions in the aqueous media.

To check the reproducibility and reliability of developed fluorescent sensors, the same experiment was repeated in triplicates. The binding abilities of Pb2+ ion with PNP@C-dot system was checked by plotting the Benesi-Hilderbrand plots by using eq. (12).
(12)
1
I −
I 0
=
1
K
(
I max
−
I 0
)
[
C +
]
2
+
1
I max
−
I 0
Where I and I0 refers to the intensity of PNP@C-dots with metal ion and without metal ion. C+ referred to the concentration of the corresponding metal cation and Imax represents the saturated emission intensity of the media. The formation of linear graph has clearly pointed towards the 2:1 stoichiometry for PNP@C-dot and metal cations and calculated value of binding constant was ∼0.02 nM.

To explore the realistic effectiveness of as synthesized fluorescent sensors, real water from different water resources i.e. distilled water, tap water, sewerage water and ground water were utilized for the analysis (Fig. 11 ). For the measurements, five different concentrations i.e. 5, 4, 3, 2 and 1 nM of Pb2+ ion was taken in water samples from different water sources in the presence of PNP@C-dot. The respective changes in the emission intensity were calculated for each sample by using linear calibration method and average recovery value of Pb2+ ion was obtained for the respective samples. All the average recovered values were found to be >90%. The results have clearly pointed towards the applicability of the formed sensor in all sources of water.

To summarize, the aim of the current work centralizes on the synthesis of C-dots without using the application of toxic and pricey chemicals. The physicochemical properties have been optimized and examined during the synthesis of C-dots. The respective effect of seed amount of Pearl Millet have been studied and found that 1.5 gm of grinded black powder from 20 gm seeds is optimized condition for obtaining C-dots with size range of 4–5 nm and quantum yield of 52%. The optical and luminescence studies have also been performed for getting insight into the optical behaviour of C-dots as function of pH ranging from 2 to 12. FTIR studies have revealed the presence of functional group over the exterior surface of C-dots and made them useful for complexation with metal ion in aqueous media. The successful synthesis and crystalline nature of C-dots has further been verified from the XRD studies. C-dots were found to be compatible towards both gram positive and negative bacterial strains with high loading range of 100–5000 mg/L. The antiproliferative (seed germination) action of developed C-dots towards Vigna-radiate seeds promotes the growth of rootlets. A. cepa chromosomal aberration assay for C-dots have further supported the compatibility towards different mitotic stages at chromosomal level. The fabricated biocompatible C-dots have been explored for the sensing of heavy metal ions toxins from aqueous media. The selectivity curve discloses the optical and luminescence sensing for Pb (II) ions in water. The lowest concentration of Pb (II) ions detected through the present sensor is of 0.18 nM. The employed strategy has not only controlled the biocompatibility parameters of C-dots but has produced direct advancements for curing environmental worries and sustainability concerns in cost effective manner. The outlook prospective of the work includes the detailed studies of the in-vivo activities of the prepared particles for enhancing their scope in biomedical applications.

Pooja Chauhan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Savita Chaudhary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Rajeev Kumar: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Savita Chaudhary is thankful to Department of Science and Technology (DST) Inspire Faculty Award [IFA CH-17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Authors are highly thankful to SAIF CIL Panjab University for technical assistance.
